don't know.

comes out.

Unfortunately, that is not what we need.

We need that articulation for several reasons.
One, we need to understand where we're going awry and being able to figure out how to affect repeatable rectifications, repeatable fixes for those issues.
And then also so that people aren't like kind of pulled along by the nose by AI systems or workflows or pipelines because we deal with them every day.
And if you have worked with AI systems at all or in content editing or other ways, you understand very quickly that there is a snow blindness that can happen very quickly.
And you're like, oh, yeah, it looks great.
It's like, no, it's a pantomime of great.
Yes.
It has the general...
When it's not actually.
When you look closely.

We live in this world where we can easily turn out stuff that to the casual eye might look good.
And then to the trained eye, they realize exactly how much it isn't good.
Right.
So we articulate that to either engineering or to other people internally, figure out how to fix or rectify the problem, make it better, and then go for it.
And so anybody that comes into the role has to sort of like have a hip pocket understanding of what good quality looks and feels like.
That's table sticks.
Right.
Just be able to look at something and go, okay, texturally, here's a problem.

Structurally or logically, here are the issues.

And basically, they hired me.
I came on as a consultant.

So I had to figure out kind of how to develop these processes, implement them, and do it all with a remote staff that maybe didn't really necessarily understand the whole idea of improving content and what we were trying to do in the first place.
So there were a few legacy folks that I had to take a
That had been there a long time.
So it was really just about, you know, sitting down.

And I mean, for me, the process is just always, you know, you're sitting down with your, with your client, you're sitting down with your customer, you're sitting down and you're figuring out, like, what is it we're even trying to do?
What is it that we want them to do?
Right?
Whether that be subscribe to a newsletter, become a customer, buy something off of a website, like, what are we doing?
Who are they?
what do they read?
What do they like?
Where do they go?
What does their life look like?
What are their pain points?
What are we trying to accomplish here?

Is it a white paper?
Is it a short video?
Is it a blog?

Who are they buying from?
What are they reading?
And where are the gaps, right?
You're doing a gap analysis.

What do we provide that they don't?

You're saying, yeah, that's great.
CoStar does X, Y, and Z, but we do the same thing and we give it to you, like, for a third of the cost, whatever that happens to be.

What's coming up in searches?
What's coming up both on an SEO standpoint, what's coming up just in AI tools, right?

Is this actually hitting?
If it is great, you continue to refine until you get it perfect.

feels unnecessary, right?
